items.title: Image classifier training diverges overnight
items.scenario: A team trains a ResNet on CIFAR-10 with SGD at η = 0.5 (default for a smaller model was 0.01). Within a few hundred steps the training loss jumps from 2.1 to NaN and accuracy collapses.
items.explanation: η was far too large for the loss curvature and batch noise. Reducing η to 0.1 or using a warmup schedule restores stable descent—classic overshoot/divergence from excessive step size.

items.title: Fine-tuning a language model crawls at η = 1e-6
items.scenario: After loading a pretrained transformer, an engineer sets η = 1e-6 for full-model fine-tuning, fearing catastrophic forgetting. Validation loss barely moves over three epochs despite correct data and gradients.
items.explanation: η is likely too small relative to gradient magnitudes at initialization. Moderate values (e.g. 1e-5 to 5e-5 with warmup) often work better; the fix is to inspect loss curves and try a short η sweep rather than assuming tiny steps are safest.

items.title: Step decay after plateau
items.scenario: A classical ML pipeline trains a logistic regression with constant η = 0.1. Loss drops quickly for 10 epochs, then flatlines with small oscillations. The engineer multiplies η by 0.1 every 5 epochs after epoch 10.
items.explanation: Early training benefited from a larger η; later stages need smaller steps to settle into the minimum without bouncing. A step schedule reduces η when progress slows—linking schedule design to training phase behavior.
